title: SIG on 2017 Income Tax Assessment for Resident Individuals
text:
STANDARD INTERPRETATION GUIDELINE 2018-06
INCOME TAX – 2017 INCOME TAX ASSESSMENT FOR RESIDENT INDIVIDUALS
This Standard Interpretation Guideline (‘SIG’) sets out Fiji Revenue and Customs Service’s policy and
operational practice in relation to the 2017 Income Tax Assessment for resident individuals.
It is issued with the authority of the Chief Executive Officer of FRCS.
All legislative references in this SIG are to the Income Tax Act 2015 (‘the ITA’), Income Tax (Rates of Tax
and Levies) Regulations 2016 and its amendments (unless otherwise stated).
This SIG will replace Draft Practice Statement No. 61/2017 – 2017 Income Tax Assessment for Resident
Individuals.
This SIG is in effect from 14 June 2018 and may need to be reviewed in the event of any relevant legislative
amendments.
CONTENTS
Executive Summary..………………………………………………………………………………………………………... 3
Introduction…………………………………………………………………………….………………………………………. 3
Legislative Analysis………………………..…………………………………………………………………….………… 4
Person(s) Covered………………….…………………………………………………………………………….…. 4
What will be taxable?………….………………………………………………………………………….………. 4
Authority for imposition of tax….…………………………………………………………………….……… 5
Tax Rates………………………………………………………………………………………………………………….. 5
How will income be taxed in 2017?……….……………………………………………………………...... 5
Chargeable Income in Period 1 only..………………………………………………………..…….………………. 7
Example 1: Employment Income…..……………....………………………………..…….………………. 7
Example 2: Employment Income……………………………….………………………………………….. 7
Chargeable Income in Period 2 only..………………………………………………………..…….………………. 8
Example 3: Employment Income…..…………… ..………………………………..…….………………. 8
Chargeable Income in Period 1 and Period 2 …………………………………………..…….………………. 9
Full Year Employment Income……………………. …………………………………………..…….………………. 9
Example 4: Employment Income (Regular Income)….……………………..…….………………. 9
1

Example 5: Employment Income (Irregular Income)….………………………………………….. 10
Part Year Employment Income……………………. …………………………………………..…….………………. 12
Example 6: Employment Income (Regular Income)………….…………………………………….. 12
Business Income……………………..…………………..…………………………………………..…….………………. 13
Example 7: Business Income……..…………………………………….…………………………………….. 13
Appendix……………………………………………………..…………………………………………..…….………………. 16
2

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
1. This SIG is issued due to the change in tax threshold with effect from 1 August 2017 and will apply to
resident individuals only for tax year 2017.
2. In the 2017/2018 National Budget Address, the Minister for Economy announced an increase in tax
threshold for resident individuals from $16,000 to $30,000.
3. The policy intention is to make the first $30,000 of chargeable income tax free with effect from 1
August 2017.
4. The change later in year 2017 created doubt on how to determine the income tax for resident
individuals for 2017 since a resident individual’s tax year is the period 1 January – 31 December (also
known as calendar year) whilst the change in tax rates was effective from 1 August 2017.
5. There are two effective tax rates that will be applied in 2017 tax year:
 one being in the period 1 January – 31 July 2017 being referred to as Period 1 Tax Rates; and
 the other in the period 1 August – 31 December 2017 being referred to as Period 2 Tax Rates.
6. The resident individual’s chargeable income and the period in which it is derived will determine which
tax brackets and which tax rate(s) will apply.
7. The formula prescribed in the Income Tax (Rates of Tax and Levies) (Amendments) Regulations 2018
(Legal Notice No. 31/2018) will be used to calculate income tax for 2017 if income is earned in both
periods.
8. Furthermore, this formula is concluded to be most viable as it is equitable and produces fair results.
These reasoning are further elaborated in this SIG through the use of examples.
9. A number of practical issues arise in relation to 2017 assessment for resident individuals, which are
considered thoroughly in this SIG.
INTRODUCTION
10. Tax year 2017 was a special year as there was a change in tax threshold and rates from 1 August. Due
to this change, there was a need for rules to determine 2017 tax assessment.
11. The rules for assessment have been addressed in the Income Tax (Rates of Tax and Levies)
(Amendments) Regulations 2018 (Legal Notice No. 31/2018).
12. The rules for determining 2017 income tax for resident individuals is summarized below.
3

Rule Employment/Income Tax Base Determination of income tax
period
1 Period 1 Chargeable  Apply Period 1 tax rates
Income
2 Period 2 Chargeable  Apply Period 2 tax rates
Income
3 Period 1 and Period 2 Chargeable  Formula will be used to determine income
Income tax
 Income Tax = 7x + 5y
12 12
13. The CEO’s views on the above rules and emerging practical issues will be clarified in this SIG.
LEGISLATIVE ANALYSIS
Person(s) Covered
14. For the purpose of this SIG, person means resident individuals which is defined in section 6 of ITA
2015. Resident individual can be an employee, sole trader, partner or a trustee.
What will be taxable?
15. A resident individual’s chargeable income will be taxable. Chargeable income is defined in section 13
of ITA 2015. It is the gross income less the total amount of allowable deductions of a person for a tax
year.
16. Gross income includes three types of income, namely; employment income, business income and
property income. It does not include exempt income or other income that has been subject to
withholding tax under section 9, 10 or 11 of ITA 2015.
17. Employment income includes salary, wages, leave pay, overtime pay, bonus, commission, directors
fees, gratuity, work condition supplements or other remuneration derived by an employee in respect
of employment which is covered in section 15 of ITA 2015.
18. Business income is the net profit earned from any business activities or any other income stated in
section 17 of ITA 2015.
19. Property income includes dividend, interest, royalty, rent or any other amount arising from use of
property which is covered in section 18 of ITA 2015. Dividend income will not be taxable as it is
exempt income under Income Tax (Exempt Income) Regulations 2016 (Part 5 – Exempt Dividends).
Interest income will not be included in computing chargeable income as it is subject to final
withholding tax.
20. Further to the above, trustee’s income which is already taxed at trust Level will not be double taxed
in the hands of the trustee.
4

Authority for Imposition of Tax
21. Section 8 of ITA governs the authority to impose tax. Section 8 (1) implies that income tax is imposed
on a person who has chargeable income for a tax year at the rate or rates prescribed by Regulations
made under ITA.
Tax Rates
22. The tax rates for Period 1 is prescribed in the Income Tax (Rates of Tax and Levies) Regulations 2016.
The rates are as follows:
Chargeable Income Income Tax Payable Social Responsibility Tax
($) ($) ($)
0-16000 Nil Nil
16,001 – 22,000 7% of excess over 16,000 Nil
22,001 – 50,000 420 + 18% of excess over 22,000 Nil
50,001-270,000 5,460 + 20% of excess over 50,000 Nil
270,001-300,000 49,460 + 20% of excess over 270,000 23% of excess over 270,000
300,001-350,000 55,460 + 20% of excess over 300,000 6,900 + 24% of excess over 300,000
350,001-400,000 65,460 + 20% of excess over 350,000 18,900 + 25% of excess over 350,000
400,001-450,000 75,460 + 20% of excess over 400,000 31,400 + 26% of excess over 400,000
450,001-500,000 85,460 + 20% of excess over 450,000 44,400 + 27% of excess over 450,000
500,001-1,00,000 95,460 + 20% of excess over 500,000 57,900 + 28% of excess over 500,000
1,000,001 + 195,460 + 20% of excess over 1,000,000 197,900 + 29% of excess over 1,000,000
Figure 1: 1 January – 31 July 2017 tax rates
23. The tax rates for Period 2 is prescribed in the Income Tax (Rates of Tax and Levies) (Amendments)
Regulations 2017 (Legal Notice No. 59/2017). The rates are as follows:
Chargeable Income Income Tax Payable Social Responsibility Tax (Inclusive of
($) ($) the Environment & Climate Adaptation
Levy)
($)
0 - 30,000 Nil Nil
30,001 - 50,000 18% of excess over $30,000 Nil
50,001 - 270,000 3,600 + 20% of excess over 50,000 Nil
270,001 - 300,000 47,600 + 20% of excess over 270,000 23% of excess over 270,000
300,001 - 350,000 53,600 + 20% of excess over 300,000 6,900 + 24% of excess over 300,000
350,001 - 400,000 63,600 + 20% of excess over 350,000 18,900 + 25% of excess over 350,000
400,001 - 450,000 73,600 + 20% of excess over 400,000 31,400 + 26% of excess over 400,000
450,001 - 500,000 83,600 + 20% of excess over 450,000 44,400 + 27% of excess over 450,000
500,001 - 1,000,000 93,600 + 20% of excess over 500,000 57,900 + 28% of excess over 500,000
1,000,001 + 193,600 + 20% of excess over 1000,000 197,900 + 29% of excess over 1,000,000
Figure 2: 1 August - 31 Dec 2017 tax rates
How will income be taxed in 2017?
24. The 2017 income tax assessment rules for resident individuals is prescribed in the Income Tax (Rates
of Tax and Levies) (Amendments) Regulations 2018 (Legal Notice No. 31/2018).
5

25. Regulation 4 provides the rules for 2017 assessment which is as follows:
[ITR 4] 2017 Income Tax Assessment
4.— (1) This regulation applies for the purposes of computing the Income Tax imposed
on a person for the tax year commencing on 1 January 2017.
(2) In this regulation, unless the context otherwise requires—
“Period 1” means the period from 1 January 2017 to 31 July 2017;
“Period 1 tax rate” means the rate or rates of Income Tax applicable to the
chargeable income of a person in Period 1;
“Period 2” means the period from 1 August 2017 to 31 December 2017;
“Period 2 tax rate” means the rate or rates of Income Tax applicable to the
chargeable income of a person in Period 2; and
“person” means a resident individual, other than a trustee where the income of
the trust is taxed in the hands of the trustee.
(3) Where a person derived chargeable income only in Period 1 and not in Period 2,
the total Income Tax for that period is computed by applying the Period 1 tax
rate.
(4) Where a person derived chargeable income only in Period 2 and not in Period
1, the total Income Tax for that period is computed by applying the Period 2 tax
rate.
(5) Where a person derived chargeable income in both Period 1 and Period 2, the
total Income Tax for both periods is computed in accordance with the following
formula—
Income Tax = 7x + 5y
12 12
where ––
“x” means the Income Tax computed by applying the Period 1 tax rate to the
chargeable income of the person for the tax year commencing on 1 January
2017;
“y” means the Income Tax computed by applying the Period 2 tax rate to the
chargeable income of the person for the tax year commencing on 1 January
2017; and
“year” means the calendar year.
26. The following are examples of CEO’s interpretation of income tax assessment for resident
individuals.
6

Chargeable income in Period 1 only
27. Regulation 4 (3) deals with a person earning chargeable income in period 1 and not period 2. The total
income tax will be calculated by applying period 1 tax rate which is illustrated in examples 1 and 2
below.
Example 1 – Employment Income
Mr. A is employed as an accountant in ZCo Ltd. His salary per annum is $48,000. He resigned in May
and will finish work on 30 June 2017. His employment income for the period 01/01/17 – 30/06/17 is
$24,000 ($48,000/12 months * 6 months).
Since the income is earned in period 1 only, apply period 1 tax rates. Also there will be no
apportionment of tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = $24,000
Step 2 Calculate income tax
Income Tax = $420 + [ 18% x ($24,000 - $22,000)]
= $420 + [18% x $2,000]
= $420 + $360
= $780
Therefore, the income tax for Mr. A is $780 for 2017.
Example 2 – Employment Income
Mrs. B works as a software developer in YCo Ltd. She is paid on an hourly basis. Her normal hourly
wage rate is $15. She works 8 hours a day and works Monday to Friday. She is paid time and half rate
for any extra hours worked. Her employment contract will end on 31/07/17 and will not be renewed.
Her employment income from 01/01/17 – 31/07/17 is as follows:
Normal Pay
Month Normal Total Days Total Hourly rate Normal Pay
Hours Hours
January 8 22 176 $15 $2640
February 8 20 160 $15 $2400
March 8 22 176 $15 $2640
April 8 20 160 $15 $2400
May 8 23 184 $15 $2760
June 8 22 176 $15 $2640
July 8 21 168 $15 $2520
Total 8 150 1200 $15 $18,000
7

Overtime Pay
Month Total Overtime Overtime rate Overtime Pay
Hours
January 16 $22.50 $360
February 20 $22.50 $450
March 14 $22.50 $315
April 22 $22.50 $495
May 16 $22.50 $360
June 14 $22.50 $315
July 12 $22.50 $270
Total 114 $22.50 $2565
She also received $3,435 bonus on 31/03/17. There is no income in period 2.
Since the income is earned in period 1 only, apply period 1 tax rates. Also there will be no
apportionment of tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = Normal Pay + Overtime Pay + Bonus
= $18,000 + $2565 + $3435
= $24,000
Step 2 Calculate income tax
Income Tax = $420 + [ 18% x ($24,000 - $22,000)]
= $420 + [18% x $2,000]
= $420 + $360
= $780
Therefore, the income tax for Mrs. B is $780 for 2017.
28. Resident individuals can also earn business income and property income in period 1 only. The
calculation of income tax will be done using period 1 tax rates as illustrated in Examples 1 and 2 above.
29. There will be no tax benefit or savings for those earning income in Period 1 only as the change in tax
rate was effective from 1 August 2017.
Chargeable income in Period 2 only
30. Regulation 4 (4) deals with a person earning chargeable income in period 2 and not period 1. The total
income tax will be calculated by applying period 2 tax rate which is illustrated in example 3 below.
8

Example 3 – Employment Income
Mr. D is employed as Human Resource Manager in ZCo Ltd. His salary per annum is $84,000. He
resumed work on 01/08/2017. His employment income for the period 01/08/17 – 31/12/17 is $35,000
($84,000/12 months * 5 months).
Since the income is earned in period 2 only, apply period 2 tax rates. Also there will be no
apportionment of tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = $35,000
Step 2 Calculate income tax
Income Tax = 18% x ($35,000 - $30,000)]
= 18% x $5,000
= $900
Therefore, the income tax for Mr. D is $900 for 2017.
31. Resident individuals can also earn business income and property income in period 2 only. The
calculation of income tax will be done using period 2 tax rates as illustrated in example 3.
32. There will be tax savings of up to $1,860 for those earning income in Period 2 only as the change in
tax rate was effective from 1 August 2017. The tax savings for different level of income will be as
follows:
Chargeable Income Tax Saving
$0 - $16,000 $0
$16,000.01 - $30,000 $0 - 1860
$30,000. 01 + 1860
The maximum tax savings is calculated as follows:
Maximum Tax Savings = Tax that will not be paid after change in tax threshold
= [$420 + 18% * ($30,000 – $22,000)]
= $1,860
Chargeable income in Period 1 and Period 2
33. Regulation 4 (5) deals with a person earning chargeable income in both period 1 and period 2. The
formula is illustrated thoroughly in examples 4 to 7 below.
Full Year Employment
Example 4 – Employment Income (Regular Income)
Mr. X is employed as an internal auditor in BCo Ltd. His salary per annum is $30,000. His employment
income for 01/01/17 – 31/07/17 was $17,500 ($30,000/12 months * 7 months) and for 01/08/17-
31/12/17 was $12,500 ($30,000/12 months * 5 months).
9

NB:
Regular Income – is the fixed income received at regular intervals. In this scenario, Mr X received
$2,500 every month. In other words, the fixed income is $2,500 and the regular interval is monthly.
There was no overtime and bonus paid to him by BCo Ltd.
Since the income is earned in both periods (period 1 and period 2), the formula will be used to
determine total income tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = Period 1 Employment Income + Period 2 Employment Income
= $17,500 + $12,500
= $30,000
Step 2 Calculate income tax for each period
x = income tax on chargeable income using Period 1 tax rates
= $420 + [18% * ($30,000 - $22,000)]
= $420 + [18% * $8,000]
= $420 + $1,440
= $1,860
y = income tax on chargeable income using Period 2 tax rates
= $0
Step 3 – Use the formula to calculate income tax
Income Tax = 7x + 5y
12 12
= 7 * $1,860 + 5 * $0
12 12
= $1,085 + $0
= $1,085
Therefore, the income tax for Mr. X is $1,085 for 2017.
Example 5 – Employment Income (Irregular income)
Mr K works as a IT support officer in B Bank Ltd. He is paid on an hourly basis. His normal hourly wage
rate is $10. He works 8 hours a day and works Monday to Friday. He is paid time and half rate for any
extra hours worked. His employment income from 01/01/17 – 31/12/17 is as follows:
10

Normal Pay
Month Normal Hours Total Days Total Hours Hourly rate Normal Pay
January 8 22 176 $10 $1760
February 8 20 160 $10 $1600
March 8 22 176 $10 $1760
April 8 20 160 $10 $1600
May 8 23 184 $10 $1840
June 8 22 176 $10 $1760
July 8 21 168 $10 $1680
August 8 23 184 $10 $1840
September 8 21 168 $10 $1680
October 8 22 176 $10 $1760
November 8 22 176 $10 $1760
December 8 21 168 $10 $1680
Total 8 259 2072 $10 $20,720
Overtime Pay
Month Total Overtime Overtime rate Overtime Pay
Hours
January 10 $15 $150
February 12 $15 $180
March 14 $15 $210
April 13 $15 $195
May 9 $15 $135
June 17 $15 $255
July 16 $15 $240
August 18 $15 $270
September 8 $15 $120
October 13 $15 $195
November 19 $15 $285
December 10 $15 $150
Total 159 $15 $2,385
He also received $2,000 bonus on 31/01/17.
NB:
Irregular Income – simply means a person receives different amount of income at different time.
Income is not fixed every pay. Income can be received at regular or irregular intervals. In this scenario,
Mr K received normal pay, overtime pay and bonus. The normal pay and overtime pay is received at
a regular interval which is monthly, however, bonus is paid only in January.
Since the income is earned in both periods (period 1 and period 2), the formula will be used to
determine total income tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
11

Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = Normal Pay + Overtime Pay + Bonus
= $20,720 + $2,385 + $2,000
= $25,105
Step 2 Calculate income tax for each period
x = income tax on chargeable income using Period 1 tax rates
= $420 + [18% * ($25,105 - $22,000)]
= $420 + [18% * $3,105]
= $420 + $558.90
= $978.90
y = income tax on chargeable income using Period 2 tax rates
= $0
Step 3 – Use the formula to calculate income tax
Income Tax = 7x + 5y
12 12
= 7 * $978.90 + 5 * $0
12 12
= $571.03 + $0
= $571.03
Therefore, the income tax for Mr. K is $571.03 for 2017.
Part Year Employment
Example 6 – Employment Income (Regular Income)
Mr. H is employed as a projects officer in ZCo Ltd. His salary per annum is $30,000. His employment
contract will end on 31/10/17 and will not be renewed. His employment income for 01/01/17 –
31/07/17 was $17,500 ($30,000/12 months * 7 months) and for 01/08/17-31/10/17 was $7,500
($30,000/12 months * 3 months).
Since the income is earned in both periods (period 1 and period 2), the formula will be used to
determine total income tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
12

Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = Period 1 Employment Income + Period 2 Employment Income
= $17,500 + $7,500
= $25,000
Step 2 Calculate income tax for each period
x = income tax on chargeable income using Period 1 tax rates
= $420 + [18% * ($25,000 - $22,000)]
= $420 + [18% * $3,000]
= $420 + $540
= $960
y = income tax on chargeable income using Period 2 tax rates
= $0
Step 3 – Use the formula to calculate income tax
Income Tax = 7x + 5y
12 12
= 7 * $960 + 5 * $0
12 12
= $560 + $0
= $560
Therefore, the income tax for Mr. H is $560 for 2017.
Example 7 – Business Income
Mr. J operates a sole trader business known as J Enterprise. The business operated for 2017 and the
total net profit after allowable deductions for tax purpose for the year was $56,000 being $21,000 for
the period 01/01/17 – 31/07/17 and $35,000 for 01/08/17-31/12/17.
Since the income is earned in both periods (period 1 and period 2), the formula will be used to
determine total income tax. The calculation of income tax is as follows:
13

Step 1 Determine the chargeable income
Chargeable Income = Period 1 business net profit + Period 2 business net profit
= $21,000 + $35,000
= $56,000
Step 2 Calculate income tax for each period
x = income tax on chargeable income using Period 1 tax rates
= $5,460 + [20% * ($56,000 - $50,000)]
= $5,460 + [20% * $6,000]
= $5,460 + $1,200
= $6,660
y = income tax on chargeable income using Period 2 tax rates
= $3,600 + [20% * ($56,000 - $50,000)]
= $3,600 + [20% * $6,000]
= $3,600 + $1,200
= $4,800
Step 3 – Use the formula to calculate income tax
Income Tax = 7x + 5y
12 12
= 7 * $6,660 + 5 * $4,800
12 12
= $3,885 + $2,000
= $5,885
Therefore, the income tax for Mr. J is $5,885 for 2017.
34. There will be tax savings of up to $775 for those earning income in Period 1 and Period 2 due to the
change in tax rate effective from 1 August 2017. The tax savings for different level of income will be
as follows:
Chargeable Income Tax Saving
$0 - $16,000 $0
$16,000.01 - $30,000 $0 - $775
$30,000. 01 + $775
14

The maximum tax savings is calculated as follows:
Maximum Tax Savings = Tax that will not be paid after change in tax threshold
= [$420 + 18% * ($30,000 – $22,000)] * 5/12
= $1,860 * 5/12
= $775
35. This SIG will be used as a guideline for 2017 tax assessment.
36. All the rules have been programmed in the tax information system to generate assessments.
37. Tax calculator to check total income tax for 2017 is also available on FRCS website. It can be
downloaded on https://www.frcs.org.fj/our-services/calculators/ .
38. For further information, you may contact us on info@frcs.org.fj.
15

APPENDIX
[ITA 6] Resident individual
(1) Subject to subsections (2) and (3), an individual is a resident individual if the individual—
(a) resides in Fiji;
(b) is domiciled in Fiji unless the individual has a permanent place of abode outside Fiji;
(c) is present in Fiji for a period of, or periods amounting in aggregate to, 183 days in any twelve-
month period; or
(d) is an employee of the Government posted abroad.
(2) An individual who is a resident individual under subsection (1) in relation to a tax year, in this section
referred to as the “current tax year”, but who was not a resident individual for the preceding tax year
is treated as a resident individual in the current tax year only for the period commencing on the day
on which the individual was first present in Fiji.
(3) An individual who is a resident individual under subsection (1) for the current tax year but who is not
a resident individual for the following tax year is treated as a resident individual in the current tax
year only for the period ending on the last day on which the individual was present in Fiji.
[ITA 8] Imposition of Income Tax and Social Responsibility Tax
(1) Subject to this Act, a tax to be known as “Income Tax” is imposed for each tax year at the rate or
rates prescribed by Regulations made under this Act on a person who has chargeable income for
the year.
(2) In addition to the Income Tax imposed under subsection (1) and subject to this Act, a tax to be known
as the “Social Responsibility Tax” is imposed for each tax year at the rate prescribed by Regulations
made under this Act on an individual, including an employee, who is liable for Income Tax for the tax
year.
(3) The Income Tax and Social Responsibility Tax imposed under subsections (1) and (2) for a tax year are
computed by applying the rate or rates of Income Tax and Social Responsibility Tax applicable to the
person prescribed by Regulations made under this Act to the chargeable income of the person for
the year, with any tax credits allowed to the person for the year subtracted from the resulting
amount.
(4) If a person is allowed more than one tax credit for a tax year, the credits are applied in the following
order—
(a) the foreign tax credit allowed under section 60;
(b) the tax credits allowed under sections 53(4), 110, and 124.
(5) Instead of taxation under subsection (1), certain classes of income, including the income of certain
classes of persons, may be subject to—
(a) tax as provided in section 9, 10 or 11; or
16

(b) withholding of tax as a final tax as provided in section 125.
(6) If, for a tax year, an individual has both chargeable income and employment income to which section
125 applies, the Income Tax payable on the chargeable income is computed according to the
following formula—
A—B
where—
A is the amount of Income Tax that would be payable on an amount of chargeable income equal to
the aggregate of the individual’s chargeable income and employment income to which section
125 applies for the year; and
B is the amount of Income Tax that would be payable on an amount of chargeable income equal to
the individual’s employment income to which section 125 applies for the year.
(7) The reference to “Income Tax” in subsection (6) includes the Social Responsibility Tax.
[ITA 15] Employment Income
(1) The following are employment income—
(a) salary, wages, or other remuneration derived by an employee in respect of employment, including
leave pay, payment in lieu of leave, overtime pay, bonus, commission, fees, gratuity, or work
condition supplements;
(b) the value of a fringe benefit, other than an exempt fringe benefit, derived by an employee in
respect of employment that is not subject to tax under the Fringe Benefits Tax;
(c) the amount of any allowance derived by an employee in respect of employment, including a cost
of living, subsistence, rent, utilities, education, entertainment, meeting or travel allowance, but
not including any allowance to the extent expended in the performance of the employee’s duties
of employment;
(d) the amount of any expenditure incurred by an employee that is reimbursed by the employer, other
than expenditure to the extent incurred on behalf of the employer in the performance of the
employee’s duties of employment;
(e) an amount derived by an employee as consideration for—
(i) the agreement by the employee to enter into employment;
(ii) the employee to agree to any conditions of employment or any changes to the employee’s
conditions of employment; or
(iii) the agreement of the employee to accept a restrictive covenant in respect of any past,
present or prospective employment;
(f) an amount derived by an employee on termination of employment, whether paid voluntarily or
under an agreement, including a redundancy payment or other compensation for loss of
employment, and golden handshake payments;
17

(g) any pension, annuity or supplement to a pension or annuity derived by an employee in respect of
employment, including a past employment;
(h) the amount of any loan, payment for an asset or services, value of any asset or services provided,
or any debt obligation released, by the company to, or in favour of, a member of the company or
an associate of a member to the extent to which the transaction is, in substance, remuneration
for services provided in any capacity to the company by the member.
(2) The value of a fringe benefit included in employment income under subsection (1)(b) or an amount
referred to in subsection (1)(h) is determined under Division 2 of Part 4.
(3) An amount or benefit is derived by an employee in respect of employment regardless of
whether it is paid or provided by— (a) the employer of the employee; (b) an associate of the
employer; or (c) third party arranger.
(4) An amount or benefit is derived by an employee even though it is paid or provided to an
associate of the employee by the employer of the employee, an associate of the employer, or a third
party arranger.
(5) In this section, “work condition supplements” includes an additional amount paid as compensation
for difficult, unpleasant, or dangerous work conditions.
[ITA 17] Business Income
(1) The following are included in the business income of a person conducting a business—
(a) the gross proceeds from the conduct of the business, including the consideration for the disposal
of trading stock and the gross fees from the provision of services;
(b) the gross revenue from the investment of the capital of the business, including dividends,
interest, royalties and rents;
(c) the net gain from—
(i) the conduct of a venture or concern in the nature of a trade, commerce, agriculture or
manufacture;
(ii) the carrying on or carrying out of a profit-making undertaking or scheme; or
(iii) the disposal of an asset, other than trading stock or an asset subject to sub-paragraph (i)
or (ii), held on revenue account by a person in carrying on a business;
(d) any other income according to ordinary concepts from the conduct of a business;
(e) any other income that a section of this Act deems to be business income.
18

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1)(c)(i) and (ii), and subject to subsection (3), the net gain arising from
the conduct of a venture or concern in the nature of a trade, commerce, agriculture or
manufacture, or in carrying on or carrying out of a profit-making undertaking or scheme is the amount
by which the gross proceeds derived by the person from the venture, concern, undertaking or scheme
exceed the expenditures or losses incurred in conducting the venture, concern, undertaking or
scheme.
(3) An expenditure or loss is taken into account in computing the net gain under subsection (2) only if
the expenditure or loss is allowed as a deduction under this Act (ignoring section 22(1)(c)).
(4) Subject to this Act, the net gain arising on disposal of an asset to which subsection (1)(c)(iii) applies is
the consideration for the disposal reduced by the cost of the asset at the time of disposal.
[ITA 18] Property income
(1) Subject to subsection (2) and section 19, the following are included in the property income of a
person—
(a) a dividend, interest, royalty, rent, natural resource amount, or other amount arising from the
provision, use or exploitation of property;
(b) the net gain from the disposal of an asset that was acquired for the purpose of disposal at a profit;
(c) a pension, charge or annuity, or any supplement to a pension, charge or annuity;
(d) a benefit paid by a retirement fund.
(2) Property income does not include income that is business or employment income.
(3) The net gain arising on disposal of an asset to which subsection (1)(b) applies is the consideration for
the disposal of the asset reduced by the cost of the asset at the time of disposal.
